prompt:
Security-engineering ticket. Our SIEM ingestion box tails the Okta System Log today with a cron + curl hack that re-reads whole hours and double-writes events after every hiccup, and the detection team is done with it. Build the replacement: a small C# library (namespace OktaSyslog) that sweeps /api/v1/logs behind a durable checkpoint, delivers each page downstream exactly once, and behaves itself against Okta's rate limiter. The xunit suite SystemLogTests.cs in this project is the acceptance contract; it, the csproj, .gitignore, and everything under docs/ are protected — don't touch them. Add your source files to this same test project (that's how our probe libraries are laid out).

Research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Okta documentation — the System Log query guide (polling vs bounded requests, what the Link headers mean, what you are and aren't allowed to do with the after cursor) plus the System Log operation reference for the exact query parameters, and Okta's rate-limit monitoring page for the exact header names and the units of the reset value. Only first-party Okta sources (developer.okta.com or Okta's own published API spec) are acceptable. docs/contract.json pins the wire contract our mock speaks and docs/official_sources.json records where it came from; if the live docs disagree with anything pinned, call that out in your summary and leave the protected files as they are. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Shape the tests compile against (internal design is yours):
- SystemLogClient(string orgBaseUrl, string apiToken, HttpClient http, Func<TimeSpan, Task> wait) with Task<LogPage> FetchPageAsync(string url) accepting a path-and-query or an absolute URL, and a LastRateLimit property (RateLimitInfo? with Limit, Remaining, ResetEpochSeconds). Every response's rate headers get parsed. On 429 the client waits until the documented reset moment — anchor the arithmetic on the response's own Date header, not the machine clock — then retries the SAME URL; three total attempts, then throw OktaRateLimitException (expose ResetEpochSeconds; message carries Okta's errorCode, never the token). When any response says the budget is spent (remaining zero), gate the NEXT request until reset the same way. All waiting goes through the injected wait — no Task.Delay, no sleeps.
- sealed record LogEvent(string Uuid, string Published, string EventType, string Severity, string? DisplayMessage, string ActorAlternateId, string OutcomeResult) — mind that two of those live inside nested objects in the wire format. sealed record LogPage(IReadOnlyList<LogEvent> Events, string? NextUrl, string? NextAfter). sealed record RateLimitInfo(int Limit, int Remaining, long ResetEpochSeconds). sealed record SweepReport(int PagesDelivered, int EventsDelivered, int DuplicatesSkipped).
- SystemLogExporter(SystemLogClient client, Func<string?> loadCursor, Action<string> saveCursor, Func<IReadOnlyList<LogEvent>, Task> deliver) with Task<SweepReport> RunSweepAsync(string since, string filter, int limit). A sweep issues the documented polling query (check the guide for which parameters a polling request must and must NOT set), resumes from the stored cursor when one exists, follows the returned next links the way the docs insist (our mock plants a marker param that dies if you rebuild URLs), stops on the first empty page, dedupes events by uuid, and advances the checkpoint for a page only after that page's deliver call has succeeded — a sink explosion mid-sweep must leave the checkpoint on the last fully delivered page so the next sweep picks up exactly there.

Acceptance: dotnet test (first run restores pinned NuGet packages). Loopback mock only — no live Okta, no real tokens.

research.product: Okta Workforce Identity Cloud — System Log API
research.interface: GET /api/v1/logs, Okta Core management REST API (official OpenAPI spec 2026.07.1)

official_sources.title: System Log query | Okta Developer
official_sources.used_for: polling vs bounded request semantics (polling = ASCENDING with empty/absent until, always returns a next link, ordered by persistence time); since/after mutually exclusive; after is system-generated for next links and must not be crafted; guidance to always follow next links instead of manually paginating with since/until; Link rel=self/rel=next header meaning; filter expression syntax (eventType eq "user.session.start")

official_sources.title: okta/okta-management-openapi-spec — Okta Admin Management API (version 2026.07.1)
official_sources.used_for: listLogEvents operation: query params since/until/after/filter/q/limit (default 100, 0..1000)/sortOrder (ASCENDING default); security SSWS apiToken or oauth2 okta.logs.read; LogEvent schema fields uuid/published/eventType/severity/displayMessage/actor/outcome/version; 429 ErrorTooManyRequests with errorCode E0000047

official_sources.title: Monitoring and troubleshooting rate limits | Okta Developer
official_sources.used_for: X-Rate-Limit-Limit (ceiling for current request), X-Rate-Limit-Remaining (requests left in window), X-Rate-Limit-Reset (reset time in UTC epoch SECONDS); 429 on exceeding a bucket; recommendation to throttle/retry with backoff anchored on the reset value

official_sources.title: Core Okta API | Okta Developer
official_sources.used_for: SSWS Authorization scheme, org base URL pattern, cursor pagination via Link headers with the instruction to follow returned Link values because cursor formats can change without notice, error envelope errorCode/errorSummary/errorLink/errorId/errorCauses

research.language_rationale: The SIEM export fleet here is .NET services; Okta documents the System Log as plain REST with Link-header pagination and header-based rate limiting, so HttpClient + System.Text.Json against the documented wire contract is the idiomatic integration (the Okta .NET management SDK wraps this same OpenAPI surface and adds nothing for a single-endpoint exporter).
